title: Why Stripe's Dashboard Hides Your Real Churn Rate
meta_description: Stripe's built-in churn number is misleading for most SaaS founders. Here's why it's wrong and how to calculate the churn rate that actually predicts revenue.
h1: Why Stripe's Built-In Dashboard Hides Your Real Churn Rate (And How to Actually Measure It)
body_markdown:
You open Stripe on a Monday morning. The dashboard says churn is 3.2%. You feel okay. Then you pull your customer list into a spreadsheet and realize 9 of your 142 paying customers didn't renew last month. That's 6.3%, nearly double what Stripe told you.

This isn't a bug. Stripe's churn number is technically correct — it's just measuring something different from what you think it is. For a solo founder trying to decide whether to hire, raise prices, or keep building, that gap between displayed churn and real churn is the difference between a healthy business and a leaky bucket you're pouring money into.

Here's what Stripe is actually showing you, why it breaks down at small scale, and how to calculate a churn rate that tells you the truth.

## What Stripe's dashboard is really counting

Stripe's billing dashboard shows a metric called **churn rate** under the subscriptions section. Under the hood, it's calculated roughly like this:

> Churned subscriptions in period ÷ Active subscriptions at start of period

That sounds right. The problem is in the definitions:

- **"Churned subscriptions"** only counts subscriptions that ended with status `canceled`. It doesn't count subscriptions that went `past_due` and silently stopped paying. It doesn't count customers who downgraded from $99/mo to $19/mo — a 80% revenue loss that registers as zero churn.
- **"Active subscriptions"** includes every subscription in a non-canceled state, including trials, $0 comped accounts, and past-due subscriptions that will almost certainly never recover.
- The period is usually the **last 30 days**, computed as a rolling window. If you had a bad week in week 2 and a good week 4, the number gets smoothed into something that looks stable.

For a mature SaaS with 10,000 customers, these distortions average out. For a founder with 80 customers, they don't. One refund processed as a cancellation instead of a refund can swing your displayed churn by more than a percentage point.

## The four things Stripe's churn number misses

### 1. Involuntary churn from failed payments

Roughly 20-40% of SaaS churn is involuntary — expired cards, insufficient funds, fraud blocks. Stripe's Smart Retries will attempt the card a few times, then mark the subscription `past_due` or eventually `unpaid`.

Here's the trap: a subscription can sit in `past_due` for weeks. During that window, Stripe still counts it as active. Your MRR number doesn't drop. Your churn rate doesn't move. But that customer is already gone — they just haven't told you yet.

If you're on $8k MRR and three customers are quietly in `past_due` at $200 each, you've actually lost $600 in MRR. Your dashboard will not reflect this for another 21 days.

### 2. Downgrade churn (revenue churn vs. logo churn)

Stripe primarily reports **logo churn** — a count of canceled subscriptions. It barely surfaces **revenue churn**, which is what actually determines whether your business grows.

Consider two months:

- **Month A:** 100 customers, 2 cancel, 0 downgrades. Logo churn 2%, revenue churn 2%.
- **Month B:** 100 customers, 0 cancel, 5 downgrade from $99 → $29. Logo churn 0%, revenue churn ~3.5%.

Stripe's dashboard will make Month B look better. A CFO would tell you Month B is worse. For a founder deciding whether your pricing page is working, this distinction is everything.

### 3. Trial-to-paid conversion pollution

If you run trials through Stripe, every trial that doesn't convert gets counted as a canceled subscription. Your churn number now blends two completely different events: customers who paid you and then left, and strangers who never paid at all.

These are different problems with different solutions. Blending them produces a number that's actionable for neither.

### 4. Annual plan accounting

Stripe measures churn on the subscription, not on recognized revenue. If a customer pays $1,200 annually in January and cancels in November, Stripe's dashboard counts them as churned in November — 11 months after you actually earned most of that revenue.

If half your base is on annual, your churn number is a lagging indicator by up to 12 months. You could be bleeding monthly customers today and see a clean dashboard because annuals from a year ago haven't come up for renewal yet.

## How to actually measure churn at $1k-$50k MRR

At your stage, you want three numbers, measured monthly, against the same cohort definition every time.

### Gross revenue churn

```
Gross Revenue Churn = (MRR lost from cancellations + downgrades + failed payments) ÷ MRR at start of month
```

This is the honest "how leaky is the bucket" number. Include:

- Full cancellations (only the MRR they were paying, not LTV)
- Downgrade deltas (old plan MRR minus new plan MRR)
- Subscriptions that have been `past_due` or `unpaid` for more than 14 days — treat them as churned until proven otherwise

Exclude trials that never converted. Track those separately as trial conversion rate.

### Net revenue churn

```
Net Revenue Churn = (Gross Churn MRR − Expansion MRR) ÷ MRR at start of month
```

Expansion MRR is upgrades, seat additions, and add-ons from existing customers. If net churn goes negative, your existing base is growing faster than it's leaving — this is the single most important number in SaaS and Stripe will never show it to you on the main dashboard.

### Customer (logo) churn, by cohort

Calculate logo churn only for customers who have been paying for **at least 60 days**. This strips out the trial-conversion noise and early-stage cancellations that reflect onboarding problems, not product-market fit problems.

## A concrete example

Let's run real numbers. Start of month:

- 120 paying customers, $14,400 MRR
- 8 customers on trial (ignore these)

During the month:

- 4 customers cancel outright (−$480 MRR)
- 2 customers downgrade from $199 to $49 (−$300 MRR)
- 3 customers go `past_due` and haven't paid in 16 days (−$350 MRR, treat as churned)
- 5 customers upgrade, adding $600 in expansion
- 12 new customers sign up ($1,440 new MRR)

**What Stripe's dashboard probably shows:** 4 ÷ 120 = 3.3% churn. Looks fine.

**Actual gross revenue churn:** ($480 + $300 + $350) ÷ $14,400 = **7.8%**

**Actual net revenue churn:** ($1,130 − $600) ÷ $14,400 = **3.7%**

The 3.3% number tells you to keep doing what you're doing. The 7.8% number tells you your retention is on fire and you need to investigate downgrades and card failures this week. Same business, two completely different mandates.

## Making this sustainable

The spreadsheet approach works for a while. Export Stripe data, categorize each MRR movement, sum by type. Most founders do this once, find the numbers alarming, and then never do it again because it takes three hours and their cofounder wants to ship a feature.

A few options to avoid that fate:

- **Dedicated metrics tool.** ChartMogul, Baremetrics, and ProfitWell all solve this properly, though pricing can be steep relative to MRR at the $1-5k range.
- **DIY with Stripe webhooks.** Write a small script that categorizes every `invoice.payment_failed`, `customer.subscription.deleted`, and `customer.subscription.updated` event. Not trivial but doable in a weekend.
- **Lightweight dashboards built specifically for bootstrappers.** This is the niche [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) was built for — it reads your Stripe account and surfaces real gross and net revenue churn (including failed payments and downgrades) without you logging into Stripe or touching a spreadsheet. Useful if you want the truth but don't want to build pipelines.

Whichever path you pick, the habit is what matters: pick one definition of churn, stick to it, and check it on the same day each month. The absolute number matters less than the trend — and no trend is readable if the underlying math keeps shifting.

## The takeaway

Stripe's dashboard isn't lying to you. It's showing you a narrow, technical definition of churn that made sense for their billing product and makes very little sense for you as an operator. Real churn at your stage includes failed payments, downgrades, and revenue weight — not just a count of `canceled` subscriptions in the last 30 days.

Measure it once properly and you'll find one of two things: your business is healthier than you thought, or it's leakier than you thought. Either answer is worth more than the comforting number on the default dashboard.
